title: Partially Hyperbolic Actions of Semisimple Lie Groups
speaker: Kurt Vinhage
abstract: Zimmer proposed the study of higher-rank semisimple group actions in the 1980's after showing certain rigidity results, especially about associated cocycles in the measurable category. In this talk, I will describe recent progress towards classifying such actions in the presence of uniformly expanding and contracting directions. Joint work with D. Damjanovic, R. Spatzier and D. Xu.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous models and weight-controlled dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from algebraic left-translation actions on homogeneous spaces and explains that their expansion, contraction, and neutral directions are read from the weights of the adjoint representation restricted to a Cartan subgroup.

proof_stages.label: Partial hyperbolicity, totality, accessibility, and the rigidity statement
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines partially hyperbolic elements with a fixed center distribution, total partial hyperbolicity for Cartan restrictions, stable/unstable accessibility, and states the rigidity theorem identifying such smooth volume-preserving ergodic actions with algebraic bi-homogeneous models up to finite cover and compact extension.

proof_stages.label: Zimmer superrigidity as measurable representation data
proof_stages.summary: Zimmer cocycle superrigidity is invoked to replace the derivative cocycle, modulo compact noise, by a measurable framing governed by a finite-dimensional representation, yielding measurable weight subbundles and metrics with prescribed exponential rates.

proof_stages.label: Regularization by invariance principles and kernel accessibility
proof_stages.summary: Using the Avila–Santamaria–Viana/Ledrappier-style invariance principle, the measurable conformal structures, invariant subbundles, and metrics are upgraded to continuous ones by exploiting volume, stable/unstable holonomies, accessibility, and zero-exponent behavior along kernels of weights.

proof_stages.label: Unwinding leafwise isometries into weight-group actions
proof_stages.summary: Kernel returns and nonstationary normal forms produce transitive isometry groups on coarse weight leaves, but because the naive homogeneous-limit maps behave like right translations, the proof passes to an orthonormal frame/principal compact bundle and uses invariant vector fields to obtain genuine leaf-parameterizing group actions.

proof_stages.label: Path-group factorization to a Lie homogeneous model
proof_stages.summary: The lifted Cartan action, compact structure group, and all constructed weight-group actions are packaged into a free path group acting transitively; after quotienting by a closed subgroup to obtain a Lie group and then by the compact group, one recovers the homogeneous-space model and reconstructs the full semisimple action.

examples.name: Rank-one hyperbolic-plane geodesic and horocycle flows
examples.note: The SL(2,R) rank-one case is used to make the adjoint-representation picture concrete: diagonal elements give exponential geodesic-flow expansion/contraction while unipotents give horocycle behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the hyperbolic-plane case by identifying the adjoint weights/eigenspaces and explaining the resulting geodesic expansion/contraction and horocycle unipotent behavior.

passes.reason: The hyperbolic-plane geodesic/horocycle flows are standard homogeneous rank-one flows used only to illustrate weights and expansion/contraction.
SL(2,R) geodesic/horocycle flows are the minimal rank-one homogeneous hyperbolic model used as a base case, not as a failure example.
The hyperbolic-plane geodesic/horocycle flows are the maximally regular constant-curvature homogeneous model used only as motivation.
Rank-one hyperbolic-plane geodesic/horocycle flows are minimal-rank homogeneous constant-curvature models used as motivating prototypes, not to refute a claim.
The hyperbolic plane geodesic/horocycle flows are homogeneous constant-curvature model flows with maximal symmetry, used only as motivation for weights and expansion.
The SL(2,R) hyperbolic-plane case is the minimal rank-one/A1 base case of the homogeneous symmetric-space examples, used only as motivation.
The hyperbolic-plane geodesic/horocycle flows are the rank-one minimal-rank constant-curvature homogeneous model used only to illustrate expansion and unipotent behavior.
Used as the simplest rank-one homogeneous model illustrating weights, with hyperbolic-plane maximal symmetry but not as a failure case.
Used as the standard homogeneous rank-one model illustrating geodesic expansion and horocycle unipotent behavior on the maximally symmetric hyperbolic plane.
used as a standard motivating homogeneous model of expansion and contraction, with the hyperbolic plane a maximally symmetric constant-curvature space
Used as a motivating homogeneous model; hyperbolic-plane geodesic/horocycle flows come from a maximally symmetric constant-curvature space.
Used as a motivating homogeneous model of expansion/contraction, and the hyperbolic plane flows come from the highly symmetric rank-one symmetric space PSL(2,R)/K.

examples.name: Brown–Fisher–Hurtado small-dimensional Zimmer rigidity
examples.note: This comparison result illustrates the opposite regime, where representation-theoretic dimension bounds force only trivial measurable dynamics rather than the hyperbolicity assumed in the main theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado result is cited and its small-dimensional Zimmer-rigidity conclusion is stated for context, without carrying out a calculation or applying the theorem to a concrete instance.

passes.reason: The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado regime is the sub-minimal-dimensional Zimmer case where only trivial representations occur, an extreme small-dimension/trivial-dynamics boundary.
The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado case is the small-dimension threshold where no nontrivial representation can appear and the action factors through a finite group.
The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado regime is the below-minimal-representation threshold where only trivial/finite dynamics can occur, and it is cited as contextual rigidity.
The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado case is the below-minimal-representation dimension extreme where only trivial/finite dynamics remain, used as contrast rather than a counterexample.
The BFH Zimmer case is the small-dimension threshold where no nontrivial representation can appear, so the dynamics collapses to finite/trivial behavior and is presented as the opposite extreme.
The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado regime is below the minimal nontrivial representation dimension, so only trivial representations/finite actions remain as the opposite extreme.
The Brown–Fisher–Hurtado case is the small-dimension threshold of Zimmer rigidity where no nontrivial representation appears and actions collapse to finite factors.
Cited as the small-dimension Zimmer extreme where only trivial representations occur and actions factor through finite groups.
Presented as the small-dimension Zimmer-rigidity extreme where only trivial representations occur and actions factor through finite groups.
presented as the low-dimensional threshold case below the smallest nontrivial representation, forcing only trivial representation data and finite actions
Presented as the opposite extreme of Zimmer phenomena, where dimension below the minimal nontrivial representation forces only trivial/finite dynamics.
Cited as the small-dimensional Zimmer case where no nontrivial representation can occur, an extremal dimension range with essentially trivial/finite dynamics.

examples.name: Geodesic flow on hyperbolic three-space and frame bundles
examples.note: The hyperbolic three-space cartoon explains why a single tangent vector determines stable horospherical geometry but a full framing is needed to parameterize the whole horosphere, clarifying the unwinding step.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the hyperbolic-three-space geodesic-flow picture, describing vectors, horospheres, contraction, and why passing from the tangent bundle to the frame bundle is needed to parameterize the leaves.

passes.reason: Hyperbolic three-space geodesic flow is a maximally symmetric homogeneous model used to show that the unit tangent bundle alone cannot globally parametrize the horospherical leaves without passing to frames.
Hyperbolic 3-space is a standard maximally symmetric model, here used to show the naive leaf-parameterizing action fails without passing to the frame bundle.
Hyperbolic three-space is a maximally symmetric constant-curvature model, used to show the obstruction to parameterizing coarse Lyapunov leaves without passing to frame bundles.
Hyperbolic three-space geodesic flow is a rank-one symmetric model used to show the naive single-leaf-parameterizing action fails unless one passes to the frame bundle.

Hyperbolic three-space and its frame bundle are maximally symmetric homogeneous objects, and the example is used to show the naive unit-tangent-bundle parametrization cannot work without passing to frames.
Hyperbolic three-space is a constant-curvature symmetric model, and it is invoked as an obstruction to the naive parameterization without passing to frame bundles.
Geodesic flow on hyperbolic 3-space is a rank-one constant-curvature homogeneous model invoked to show that parametrizing horospherical leaves fails without passing to the frame bundle.
Used to show the naive horosphere-parameterizing action fails without passing to a frame bundle, in the maximally symmetric hyperbolic-space model.
Used to show the obstruction to parametrizing coarse Lyapunov leaves without passing from the tangent bundle to the frame bundle, in a highly symmetric hyperbolic-space model.
used to show the naive tangent-bundle parametrization fails without passing to frames, while hyperbolic three-space is a symmetric constant-curvature model
Used to show the obstruction to a naive single leaf-parameterizing action without passing to frame bundles; hyperbolic three-space is a maximally symmetric model.
Used to show the obstruction to parameterizing horospherical leaves without passing to a frame bundle, while hyperbolic three-space is a constant-curvature symmetric space.
